location.origin: Seattle, Washington -- born 1983; youngest artist in the engine; first millennial-generation musician; attended UW pre-college music program at 14; exported to Manhattan School of Music at 16
location.formative: Manhattan School of Music (NYC); Thelonious Monk Competition winner (2001, age 18); Terence Blanchard's band (joined at 19); Blue Note Records debut (2008)
location.career: New York City (permanent base) -- Blue Note Records debut (Invisible Cinema, 2008); ECM Records (Arborescence, 2013); Little Big (Ropeadope Records); active performer and recording artist; has not returned to Seattle as professional base

theory_nodes.concept: Post-genre jazz piano vocabulary (quartal voicings as inherited vocabulary, slash chords and ambiguous tonality, metric modulation and floating time, indie rock textures as compositional resource, electronic ambient spaces, tritone substitution as unconscious inheritance)
examples: Aaron Parks -- Invisible Cinema (Blue Note, 2008): post-bop + indie rock textures + non-jazz compositional sensibility; synthesis of Hancock/Jarrett with Radiohead/Bjork
Aaron Parks -- Arborescence (ECM, 2013): solo piano, ECM spaciousness; reverb as instrument, silence as compositional element
Little Big -- jazz/indie rock integration: guitar-driven textures (Greg Tuohey), layered production, song-form structures alongside improvisation
Brad Mehldau -- Largo: parallel post-genre jazz piano deployment; classical/rock/jazz synthesis
Vijay Iyer -- contemporary jazz piano with electronic/hip-hop influence; generational peer to Parks
theory_nodes.description: Post-genre jazz piano vocabulary is the contemporary synthesis that extends the modal jazz revolution (degree 13) into territory where jazz, indie rock, electronic, and ambient music merge. Core techniques: (1) Quartal voicings as inherited vocabulary -- three-generation cycle from Tyner's innovation to standard vocabulary (Seales, degree 13) to unconscious inheritance (Parks). (2) Slash chords and ambiguous tonality -- upper-structure chords over non-root bass notes (Bb/C, D/F#) producing floating harmonic ambiguity that resists resolution; the space between jazz functional harmony and indie rock triadic simplicity. (3) Metric modulation and floating time -- superimposing new tempi derived from subdivisions of existing pulse; time as elastic rather than complex. (4) Indie rock textures as compositional resource -- sustained guitar chords with delay, tremolo, shoegaze washes informing piano voicings and production aesthetic. (5) Electronic ambient spaces -- reverb and delay as structural elements; ECM production philosophy extended into explicitly electronic territory. (6) Tritone substitution as unconscious inheritance -- the degree-0 thread reaches its endpoint: from innovative device (bebop) to standard vocabulary (modal) to absorbed language (post-genre).

ancestors: Modal jazz piano vocabulary -- quartal voicings, extended chords, Lydian Dominant (degree 13, Seales)
Bebop vocabulary -- chromatic approach, tritone substitution (degree 12, Lanphere)
Hard bop piano voicings (degree 6, Bown)
Stride piano (degree 5, Berry)
ECM aesthetic -- Manfred Eicher production philosophy (degrees 1, 7, 8)
Indie rock harmony -- Radiohead, My Bloody Valentine, Explosions in the Sky
Electronic ambient -- Brian Eno, Aphex Twin ambient works
genealogy.relationship: Post-genre jazz piano descends from modal jazz (degree 13) through a specific generational path: Parks inherited quartal voicings and modal scales as baseline vocabulary (not innovation) and extended them by integrating non-jazz textures -- indie rock guitar voicings, electronic ambient production, and song-form compositional structures. The piano theory branch extends to nine nodes at degree 14, widening its lead as the richest branch. The fourteenth chromaticism deployment: post-genre chromaticism -- atmospheric movement between distantly related key centers for emotional narrative rather than theoretical logic. Chromaticism after theory.

theory_nodes.concept: The prodigy-export pipeline (institutional identification at UW pre-college -> conservatory export to MSM at 16 -> Monk Competition launch at 18 -> professional apprenticeship in Blanchard band -> Blue Note debut -> global career; the Quincy Jones pipeline modernized)
examples: Aaron Parks -- UW pre-college at 14, MSM at 16, Monk Competition at 18, Blanchard at 19, Blue Note at 25; the complete institutional export pipeline
Quincy Jones (degree 0) -- Garfield High School, Lionel Hampton band, national touring; the 1950s bandstand export pipeline (comparison)
Thelonious Monk International Jazz Piano Competition -- the institutional trigger replacing the bandstand identification; judges, rules, organizational structure
Terence Blanchard -- post-Blakey bandleader-as-mentor model; the working group as professional apprenticeship
Manhattan School of Music -- elite jazz conservatory alongside Juilliard, Berklee, NEC; the destination institution for exported prodigies
theory_nodes.description: The prodigy-export pipeline is the institutional mechanism that identifies exceptional talent in regional cities, exports it to national training centers, and launches it into global careers. The pipeline has been institutionalized across fifty years: where the 1950s pipeline (Jones, degree 0) ran through nightclubs and touring bands (informal identification -> bandstand apprenticeship -> hired by touring leader -> career), the 2000s pipeline (Parks, degree 14) runs through pre-college programs, conservatories, and international competitions (institutional identification -> conservatory admission -> competition win -> professional apprenticeship -> label debut -> career). The Monk Competition is the institutional trigger that the Hampton hiring used to be. The pipeline exports talent younger (16 vs. ~18) because it can identify talent earlier. The Institutional Anchor (Seales, degree 13) and the Prodigy-Export (Parks, degree 14) are structurally complementary: one anchors the local institution, the other is launched by it.

ancestors: The Export pattern -- Jones (degree 0); Seattle-born talent leaves for career center
Experiential teaching / bandstand identification -- Standifer (degree 4)
Institutional identification -- UW pre-college program, connected to Seales (degree 13)
Competition circuit -- Monk Institute as formalized talent identification
genealogy.relationship: Two exports in fifteen degrees. Jones (0, bandstand pipeline, 1950s) and Parks (14, institutional pipeline, 2000s). Same fundamental pattern -- Seattle talent leaves for career center and does not return -- but the mechanism has been formalized. The transition from informal to institutional export parallels the Standifer-to-Seales pedagogical arc (experiential to doctoral) documented at degree 13. Both arcs track the professionalization of jazz: from informal art form to formal academic and competitive institution.

theory_nodes.concept: Piano lineage -- four eras (stride/cocktail -> hard bop/orchestral -> modal/contemporary -> post-genre; autodidact -> undergraduate -> doctoral -> conservatory-prodigy; the full arc of jazz piano evolution, education, and city-relationship in Seattle)
examples: Overton Berry (degree 5) -- stride/cocktail, self-taught, The Persistence, energy 2, 1940s-2020s
Patti Bown (degree 6) -- hard bop/orchestral, Seattle University, The Erasure, energy 3, 1950s-1970s
Marc Seales (degree 13) -- modal/contemporary, UW DMA, The Institutional Anchor, energy 2, 1990s-present
Aaron Parks (degree 14) -- post-genre, UW pre-college/MSM, The Prodigy-Export, energy 2, 2000s-present
theory_nodes.description: Four Seattle jazz pianists across four eras document the full evolution of jazz piano style, jazz education, and artist-city relationships. Berry (degree 5): stride and cocktail tradition, self-taught, the oldest vocabulary rooted in 1920s-1940s swing language, The Persistence (never left). Bown (degree 6): hard bop and orchestral approach, Seattle University undergraduate training, the 1950s-1960s vocabulary of harmonic density, The Erasure (exported, succeeded, forgotten). Seales (degree 13): modal and contemporary language, UW doctoral training, the post-Coltrane vocabulary of open voicings and theoretical awareness, The Institutional Anchor (native, rooted, teaching). Parks (degree 14): post-genre synthesis of jazz/indie/electronic, UW pre-college to MSM conservatory, quartal voicings as inherited vocabulary extended with non-jazz textures, The Prodigy-Export (identified, shipped out at 16, never returned). The educational progression now maps four paths: autodidact -> undergraduate -> doctorate -> conservatory-prodigy. The city-relationship progression adds a fourth pattern: persistence (never left) -> erasure (left, forgotten) -> anchor (stayed, taught) -> prodigy-export (identified, shipped out). The piano theory branch extends to nine nodes at degree 14.

ancestors: Stride piano vocabulary (degree 5, Berry)
Hard bop piano voicings (degree 6, Bown)
Modal piano vocabulary (degree 13, Seales)
Bebop piano vocabulary (Bud Powell, referenced at degree 12)
Modal piano innovation (Tyner, Hancock, Corea, Jarrett -- national lineage)
Post-genre synthesis (Mehldau, Iyer -- national context)
genealogy.relationship: The four pianists form a direct lineage within the engine: Berry's stride vocabulary was the standard Seattle jazz piano sound for decades; Bown's hard bop approach represented modernization in the 1950s-1960s; Seales's modal vocabulary represents the institutional-academic peak; Parks's post-genre synthesis represents the youngest generation. The educational escalation (self-taught -> undergraduate -> doctoral -> conservatory-prodigy) parallels stylistic evolution and maps the professionalization of jazz. The city-relationship progression (persistence -> erasure -> anchor -> export) maps four distinct ways Seattle relates to its piano talent. Nine piano theory nodes -- extends lead over saxophone/reed (seven) as richest branch. The piano group extends from a trio to a quartet.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- post-genre floating (fourteenth deployment; blue note floating between jazz and rock semantics, deliberately unresolved minor-over-major ambiguity)
examples: Aaron Parks -- minor-over-major ambiguity in post-genre piano voicings; the b3 floats between blues inflection, Dorian modal pitch, and indie-rock minor chord
Radiohead -- rock blue notes deployed in harmonically sophisticated contexts; OK Computer and Kid A as influence on jazz aesthetics
Brad Mehldau -- jazz/rock blue-note synthesis in piano voicings; parallel deployment to Parks
Marc Seales (degree 13) -- modal integration where blue notes become defining pitches of the mode; the predecessor deployment
theory_nodes.description: Fourteenth deployment of the blue note thread. In post-genre jazz, the blue note (b3, b7) floats between jazz and rock semantics. Is the minor third a blues inflection (the traditional jazz reading), a Dorian modal pitch (the modal jazz reading from degree 13), or an indie-rock minor chord (the rock reading)? Parks leaves the question deliberately unresolved. The blue note belongs fully to neither tradition -- it occupies the post-genre space between. This extends the modal integration documented at degree 13 (where the blue note was absorbed into the mode itself as a defining pitch) into genre-ambiguous territory (where the blue note's semantic identity depends on the listener's frame of reference). The post-genre blue note is the most ambiguous deployment in the engine: not wrong (blues tradition), not structural (modal jazz), not purely harmonic (rock) -- simultaneously all three.

ancestors: Blue note -- modal integration (degree 13, Seales)
Blue note -- all thirteen prior deployments (degrees 0-13)
Rock/indie rock blue-note traditions (Radiohead, Radiohead-influenced jazz)
genealogy.relationship: Fourteen deployments across fifteen degrees. The post-genre blue note is the most ambiguous mechanism: the blue note's semantic identity floats between jazz, modal, and rock readings depending on context and listener. Extends modal integration (degree 13, where the blue note IS the mode) by adding rock semantics (where the blue note IS a minor chord). Fourteen deployments: bass-line (0), guitar (1), vocal (3), trumpet (4), piano crush (5), altered voicings (6), trombone slide (7), bass fundamental (8), sax voice (9), alto cool bend (10), flute bent note (11), bebop chromatic approach (12), modal integration (13), post-genre floating (14).

seattle_connection: Born in Seattle, 1983 -- native-born musician exported through the institutional pipeline at age 16. Attended UW pre-college music program at 14 (connecting to Seales, degree 13, who directs UW jazz studies). Left Seattle for Manhattan School of Music at 16. Won the Monk Competition at 18. Has not returned to Seattle as a professional base. The Prodigy-Export pattern: native-born, identified as exceptional before 16, exported to a major training center before completing secondary education, career based elsewhere. The second export in the engine after Jones (degree 0). The institutional pipeline modernizes the bandstand pipeline: pre-college program replaces nightclub, competition replaces gig, conservatory replaces touring band. Energy-2 profile -- ambient warmth, atmospheric, spacious. Ninth energy-2 artist.

theory_genealogy: Post-genre jazz piano vocabulary (quartal voicings as inherited vocabulary, slash chords, ambiguous tonality, metric modulation, indie rock textures, electronic ambient, tritone substitution as unconscious inheritance) -- ninth node on the piano theory branch, extends lead as richest; inherits from modal jazz (degree 13) and extends into post-genre territory

The prodigy-export pipeline (institutional identification -> conservatory export -> competition launch -> professional apprenticeship -> label debut -> global career) -- the Quincy Jones export pipeline modernized; complements the Institutional Anchor (degree 13)

Piano lineage (Berry/stride -> Bown/hard bop -> Seales/modal -> Parks/post-genre; autodidact -> undergraduate -> doctoral -> conservatory-prodigy) -- four eras, four educational paths, four city-relationship patterns; quartet extends the trio
Blue note -- fourteenth deployment; post-genre floating between jazz, modal, and rock semantics; the most ambiguous deployment

key_albums.title: Invisible Cinema
key_albums.label: Blue Note
key_albums.status: acclaimed debut -- one of the most promising jazz piano debuts of the decade
key_albums.significance: Blue Note Records debut. Synthesis of post-bop jazz harmony, indie rock textures (layered guitars, ambient washes), and compositional sensibility drawn from Radiohead and Bjork alongside Hancock and Jarrett. The most storied label in jazz history positioned Parks as a generational voice. Third Blue Note connection in the engine. First Blue Note debut album.

key_albums.title: Arborescence
key_albums.label: ECM
key_albums.status: solo piano -- ECM spaciousness and contemplation
key_albums.significance: ECM Records solo piano album. Connected Parks to the ECM tradition of spacious, reverberant piano recordings (Jarrett Koln Concert, Bley solo work). ECM thread extends to four artists (Frisell 1, Priester 7, Peacock 8, Parks 14). Manfred Eicher's production philosophy of space, silence, and acoustic clarity.

key_albums.title: Little Big recordings
key_albums.label: Ropeadope
key_albums.status: jazz/indie rock integration -- post-genre ensemble project
key_albums.significance: Little Big (Parks, Tuohey, Morgan, Gilmore) explicitly bridges jazz and indie rock. Guitar-driven textures, layered production, song-form structures alongside improvisation. Ropeadope Records -- Philadelphia-based independent label known for genre-crossing jazz. First Ropeadope appearance in the engine. The fullest expression of Parks's post-genre aesthetic.

degree_neighbors.name: Marc Seales
degree_neighbors.relationship: Piano lineage (third chair) and UW connection. Seales directs UW jazz studies; Parks attended UW pre-college. The Institutional Anchor and the Prodigy-Export are structurally complementary -- one anchors the local institution, the other is launched by it. Both Seattle-born, both connected to UW, one rooted and one exported.

degree_neighbors.name: Quincy Jones
degree_neighbors.relationship: The export pipeline. Jones is the first export (1950s, bandstand pipeline); Parks is the second (2000s, institutional pipeline). Both Seattle-born, both left as teenagers, both achieved global careers, neither returned. The pipeline has been institutionalized across fifty years.

degree_neighbors.name: Gary Peacock
degree_neighbors.relationship: ECM connection. Parks recorded Arborescence for ECM; Peacock is the engine's primary ECM artist. Shared production philosophy of space, silence, acoustic clarity. ECM thread extends to four artists.

degree_neighbors.name: Bill Frisell
degree_neighbors.relationship: Post-genre connection. Frisell pioneered non-jazz texture integration (Americana, country, noise, ambient); Parks extends this into the next generation (indie rock, electronic, ambient). Both are atmospheric energy-2 artists occupying ambient space.

degree_neighbors.name: Overton Berry
degree_neighbors.relationship: Piano lineage (first chair). Berry's stride vocabulary is three generations removed from Parks's post-genre language. Four-pianist arc: autodidact -> undergraduate -> doctorate -> conservatory-prodigy. Both Seattle-born.

garfield_thread.degree_0: Quincy Jones -- attended late 1940s, class of 1950. Arranger/producer. Left Seattle.
garfield_thread.degree_3: Ernestine Anderson -- attended late 1940s, same era as Jones. Vocalist. Left and returned.
garfield_thread.degree_4: Floyd Standifer -- Central District post-WWII, educational path not confirmed as Garfield. Multi-instrumentalist and educator. Never left.
garfield_thread.degree_5: Overton Berry -- Central District native, self-taught. No institutional music education. Autodidact path.
garfield_thread.degree_6: Patti Bown -- Central District native, Seattle University (not Garfield). Formal training via Jesuit university.
garfield_thread.degree_7: Julian Priester -- NOT a Garfield product. DuSable High School (Chicago) under Captain Dyett. Cornish College of the Arts faculty.
garfield_thread.degree_8: Gary Peacock -- NOT a Garfield product. Burley, Idaho. Westlake College (LA). U.S. Army (Germany).
garfield_thread.degree_9: Hadley Caliman -- NOT a Garfield product. Idabel, Oklahoma. Los Angeles jazz scene. Cornish College of the Arts faculty.
garfield_thread.degree_10: Bud Shank -- NOT a Garfield product. Dayton, Ohio. University of North Carolina. Stan Kenton Orchestra. Los Angeles / Pacific Jazz.
garfield_thread.degree_11: Joe Brazil -- NOT a Garfield product. Detroit, Michigan. Detroit jazz scene. Settled in Central District as salon-model educator.
garfield_thread.degree_12: Don Lanphere -- NOT a Garfield product. Wenatchee, Washington. Northwestern University. NYC 52nd Street bebop scene.
garfield_thread.degree_13: Marc Seales -- NOT confirmed as Garfield product. Seattle native. University of Washington (DMA). Seventh consecutive non-Garfield degree. First UW-complete pipeline.
garfield_thread.degree_14: Aaron Parks -- NOT confirmed as Garfield product. Born Seattle 1983. UW pre-college at 14, MSM at 16. Eighth consecutive non-Garfield degree. Exported before completing high school in Seattle.

cultural_patterns.institution_type: the prodigy-export -- native-born, identified as exceptional at UW pre-college at 14, exported to MSM conservatory at 16, Monk Competition at 18, Blanchard band at 19, Blue Note debut at 25, career permanently based in NYC; the most institutionally mediated export pattern in the engine
